title: Type and ownership visual grammar
summary: Type positions use compact caret, optional, expected, array, string, and smart-pointer forms while expression operators keep their ordinary meaning.
details: Pointer declarator stars become carets only in type positions; multiplication and dereference stars remain stars.
std::optional<T> becomes T?, std::expected<T, E> becomes T?E, and nested std::array<T, N> becomes [N]T.
unique_ptr<T> and shared_ptr<T> are presented as T^; a custom deleter is retained as a comma-delimited tilde arm.
const char* becomes CString and additional pointer levels remain carets; string, string_view, and configured zero-terminated string aliases share the string color.
Integer and floating C spellings normalize to the configured short aliases such as u32, i32, f32, and f64.
CUDA complex spellings follow DEC-005: cuFloatComplex and cuComplex become CUDA-colored cf32, and cuDoubleComplex becomes CUDA-colored cf64.
std::move and std::forward retain their operation names but are ownership-transfer colored; cpy marks an intentionally heavy by-value copy and copy joins the mutation/ownership color family.
Casts collapse to dollar aliases and pointer stars inside cast type arguments become carets.
Unsupported auto&& variable declarations stay raw, while forwarding references in parameters and range-for bindings have dedicated spellings.

provenance.note: Type normalization and semantic chunks are centralized here for overlay paths.
Raw-line declarators, CString cases, casts, and ownership aliases cover non-overlay positions.

evidence.reference: tests/frontend_spec.lua type, nested-template, pointer, cast, and ownership cases
evidence.claim: Nested and top-level type transformations are pinned.
Raw and overlay string/CString paths agree and avoid literal corruption.

questions: Why does a pointer type use caret without changing multiplication?
How are optional, expected, arrays, and smart pointers rendered?

last_verified.method: Type-normalization code and focused raw/overlay/nested-type assertions reviewed.
